title: Hanson offers an ‘eternity of pain’
full_text:
Angus Taylor has warned a One Nation government would create an “eternity of pain” for Australians and deliver even worse economic outcomes than the Albanese government, likening Pauline Hanson’s populist party to the Greens in his fiercest critique since becoming Liberal leader.
The Opposition Leader said One Nation’s unfunded policies would increase inflation, send interest rates 3 per cent higher, cost mortgage holders $20,000 a year, triple the debt, and cause a sovereign debt crisis requiring cuts to pensions and Medicare.
In a major speech to the Sydney Institute on Thursday evening, Mr Taylor warned Australians were becoming poorer under Labor due to bigger government, sluggish productivity, high immigration, support for net zero and a the rollout of a union-endorsed industrial relations agenda.
“Labor expands government, which slows the economy, reducing revenue,” Mr Taylor said. “So it levies new taxes on investment, saving, and aspiration. And this slows the economy further, reducing revenue further still. Labor has put our economy into a death loop.” But he warned the future would be even worse under a government led by Senator Hanson, likening One Nation to the Greens while declaring the party would “send us broke” if it won an election.
Mr Taylor’s speech came as Senator Hanson travelled to Britain this week to meet with far-right celebrities including former Neighbours actor Holly Valance and the controversial anti-Islam podcaster Tommy Robinson.
With the Coalition grappling over how to campaign against a surging One Nation that is rivalling Labor in the polls, Mr Taylor did not take a personal swipe at Senator Hanson but tore down her party’s answers to Australia’s economic problems and argued she did not have a credible team around her.
He also empathised with many voters turning to One Nation, describing them as people who have done “everything right” but are weighed down by an economy not delivering for them.
“Many have lost hope,” he said, according to a copy of his speech. “They look left at a government that lies to them. They look right at an opposition that has too many times let them down. I understand why some Australians think the way out is to blow the place up.
“But to those who feel like lighting a match, believe me when I say, that a moment of satisfaction isn’t worth the eternity of pain that will follow.
“One Nation claims to offer a way out of our national malaise. In reality, they would only make things worse.” Describing One Nation as a “column of smoke”, Mr Taylor said the party was long on rhetoric but short on substance.
He rejected Senator Hanson’s claim One Nation had consistent values since she founded the party in the 1990s, highlighting the divergent views of Queensland senator Malcolm Roberts and new Farrer MP David Farley.
“One Nation’s offering is a random grab bag of poorly defined, contradictory, and constantly changing positions that leave no clear sense of who they are or what they stand for,” he said. “Their longest serving MP thinks the United States is the ‘world’s greatest terrorist organisation’, and their newest MP is already voting with the Greens and teals.” While Mr Taylor said commonwealth spending needed to be curtailed to fix the nation’s economic woes, he accused One Nation of planning to “double down” on big government intervention. “But with even less of a concern than Labor about how to make the numbers add up,” Mr Taylor said. “Their top four financial commitments alone could cost the budget in the order of a trillion dollars over a decade. And they have no clear or credible plan for how they’d pay for these commitments.
“One Nation’s promise to abolish parts of the bureaucracy would cover perhaps just one 15th of these commitments.
“Of course, it’s easy to make promises when you aren’t a party of government. One Nation is a lot like the Greens in that regard.” Mr Taylor said One Nation’s unfunded policies would “generate a surge in inflation requiring the RBA to raise interest rates by around 3 percentage points”.
“That would add around $20,000 a year in interest to the average new mortgage. And that’s on top of the $30,000 a year Labor has already lumped mortgage holders with,” he said.
“Our national debt, already nearing $1 trillion, would be on track to nearly triple. Rising rates would then create a compounding feedback loop. Down that road lies a sovereign debt crisis.
“Their only alternative is deeper cuts to essential services – pensions and Medicare chief among them. That’s why I warn that an eternity of pain would follow a One Nation government.” Mr Taylor is basing his analysis off One Nation’s four most expensive policies including supporting the Coalition’s policy to index tax thresholds, income splitting, raising defence spending to 3.5 per cent of gross domestic product and “net-zero migration”.
A spokesman for Senator Hanson dismissed Mr Taylor’s attack. “The Coalition had almost 10 years to end the real cost-of-living pain caused to Australian households and businesses by net zero and did nothing,” the spokesman said. “The Coalition had almost 10 years to end the real housing pain caused by mass immigration and did nothing. There’s imagined pain, and then there’s real pain. Who’s responsible for the real pain?” The Liberal leader also faces his own questions from Labor over balancing his own policies, with Jim Chalmers accusing the Coalition of having a $544bn black hole. This is partly predicated on Mr Taylor vowing to wind back Labor’s budget tax hikes, increase defence spending to 3 per cent of GDP and indexing tax thresholds at a cost of more than $200bn over a decade. Mr Taylor is also promising to cut migration to lower levels than Labor, which will impact the budget.
The Australian reported last month leading economists were concerned that One Nation’s policies would not help the fight against inflation and provide the most help for the wealthy.
Among One Nation’s cost-of-living measures is income splitting for couples with dependent children, halving the fuel excise for three years, raising the tax-free threshold for self-funded retirees to $35,000 and removing the excise on beer and spirits in venues. It also has proposals to immediately cut electricity prices by 20 per cent, allow pensioners to work more without cutting payments, and an unspecified increase to the Medicare rebate to “better remunerate GPs and promote bulk billing”.
While One Nation has not costed its policies – falsely claiming the PBO was only available for parties with five or more seats in the lower house – economists put the price tag on its fuel excise policy at about $10bn a year while the alcohol tax policy would cost about $2bn a year.
One Nation claims it will offset spending through a $90bn-a-year plan to cut “government waste”, despite modelling by former Liberal senator Gerard Rennick showing income splitting alone could cost as much as $70bn over 10 years, while the price of a promised defence investment uplift would be hundreds of billions of dollars over a decade.
